title: Why some languages survive in households long after they disappear from the streets
summary: Explore why minority languages persist inside homes even when they vanish from public life.
topic: Why some languages survive in households long after they disappear from the streets
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring why certain minority languages persist inside homes even after they have disappeared from shops, schools, and public signage. Use at least one international example (e.g. Quechua in Lima households, Welsh in Welsh valley homes, or Yiddish in Brooklyn kitchens). Include a cleft sentence ('What keeps…'), a concession ('Granted, …'), and a passive construction. Take a clear position on whether household survival is enough to call a language 'alive'.
hintStarters: In the Lima suburb of San Juan de Miraflores, Quechua is…
What keeps a language breathing inside four walls is not grammar but…
Granted, a language heard only at the dinner table is…
The question worth asking is whether survival at home counts as survival at all…

hintVocab.meaningVi: chuyển đổi ngôn ngữ trong hội thoại
truyền lại qua các thế hệ
ngôn ngữ thân mật / phong cách thân mật
tồn tại / tiếp tục sống sót
tạo ra một không gian riêng
tiếng địa phương / ngôn ngữ bản địa

sampleText:
In the Lima suburb of San Juan de Miraflores, Quechua is not spoken in the market, not used on government forms, and not heard on the radio. Yet in certain kitchens, grandmothers still address their grandchildren in it — and those grandchildren, even without replying in kind, grow up knowing its rhythms. The language has receded from every public surface and survived, somehow, behind a door.

What keeps a language breathing inside four walls is not grammar but intimacy. Quechua, Welsh in the households of the Rhondda Valley, Yiddish in Brooklyn apartments — each of these has been displaced from public life by a dominant language better suited to commerce and bureaucracy. Yet they endure in bedrooms and kitchens because they carry something the prestige language cannot replicate: the exact emotional register of a parent scolding, a grandmother comforting, a joke that only works in the original. Language, at its most stubborn, is a set of feelings before it is a set of rules.

Granted, a language heard only at the dinner table is a fragile thing. Intergenerational transmission requires not merely exposure but use: a child who understands Quechua but answers in Spanish is already one step from the edge. Linguists describe this as passive competence, and it is no guarantee of survival — within a generation or two, the passive knowledge tends to fade unless something gives the language a reason to be spoken aloud. Welsh reversed this drift partly because road signs and school curricula gave it a public function again; Quechua in urban Peru has no such institutional scaffold.

Even so, household survival is not nothing. It is, in fact, the seed from which revivals have been grown. Hebrew was kept alive largely in liturgical and domestic contexts for centuries before it was revitalised as a spoken language in the late nineteenth century. What was preserved in those domestic rituals — vocabulary, syntax, the cadence of prayer — gave revivalists something concrete to build on. A language that has been entirely replaced, by contrast, can only be reconstructed, and reconstruction is always poorer than the original.

The honest answer, then, is that a language spoken only at home is neither alive nor dead — it is dormant. Whether it remains dormant or wakes depends less on the language itself than on whether the community decides, collectively, to carve out a space for it beyond the kitchen. The households that hold on to minority languages are not preserving museum exhibits; they are holding a door open. Whether anyone chooses to walk through it is a different question entirely.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to front the key explanation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What keeps a language breathing inside four walls is not grammar but intimacy' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để đặt điều quan trọng nhất (intimacy) ở cuối câu, tạo nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What keeps a language breathing inside four walls is not grammar but intimacy' uses a what-cleft to delay the key noun until the end of the clause, placing maximum weight on 'intimacy'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to background the agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Hebrew was kept alive largely in liturgical and domestic contexts' dùng bị động vì tác nhân không quan trọng bằng kết quả — ngôn ngữ được giữ sống — và bị động giúp nhấn mạnh kết quả đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Hebrew was kept alive largely in liturgical and domestic contexts' uses the passive to foreground the language (subject) rather than the people who maintained it — a common B2 move when the action matters more than the actor.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở khu ngoại ô Lima là San Juan de Miraflores, tiếng Quechua không được nói trong chợ, không dùng trên biểu mẫu nhà nước, và không nghe trên đài phát thanh. Nhưng trong một số căn bếp, các bà ngoại vẫn nói chuyện với cháu bằng tiếng đó — và những đứa cháu đó, dù không trả lời bằng tiếng Quechua, lớn lên quen với nhịp điệu của nó. Ngôn ngữ đã lùi dần khỏi mọi bề mặt công cộng và vẫn tồn tại, theo cách nào đó, sau một cánh cửa.

Điều giữ cho một ngôn ngữ còn thở trong bốn bức tường không phải là ngữ pháp mà là sự thân mật. Tiếng Quechua, tiếng Welsh trong các hộ gia đình ở thung lũng Rhondda, tiếng Yiddish trong những căn hộ Brooklyn — mỗi ngôn ngữ này đã bị thay thế khỏi đời sống công cộng bởi một ngôn ngữ thống trị phù hợp hơn cho thương mại và hành chính. Nhưng chúng tồn tại trong phòng ngủ và bếp vì chúng mang thứ mà ngôn ngữ có uy thế không thể sao chép được: chính xác phong cách cảm xúc của cha mẹ mắng, bà ngoại vỗ về, một câu đùa chỉ có nghĩa khi nói bằng bản gốc. Ngôn ngữ, ở mức cứng đầu nhất, là tập hợp cảm xúc trước khi là tập hợp quy tắc.

Công nhận, một ngôn ngữ chỉ được nghe tại bàn ăn là điều mong manh. Truyền lại qua các thế hệ đòi hỏi không chỉ tiếp xúc mà còn cần sử dụng: một đứa trẻ hiểu tiếng Quechua nhưng trả lời bằng tiếng Tây Ban Nha đã đứng ở bờ vực rồi. Các nhà ngôn ngữ học gọi đây là năng lực thụ động, và không có gì đảm bảo sự tồn tại — trong một hoặc hai thế hệ, kiến thức thụ động có xu hướng mờ dần trừ khi có gì đó cho ngôn ngữ lý do để được nói thành tiếng. Tiếng Welsh đã đảo ngược xu hướng này một phần vì biển đường và chương trình học trao cho nó chức năng công cộng trở lại; tiếng Quechua ở thành thị Peru không có giàn giáo thể chế như vậy.

Dẫu vậy, sự tồn tại trong gia đình không phải là không có gì. Thực ra, đó là hạt giống từ đó những cuộc hồi sinh đã nảy mầm. Tiếng Hebrew được giữ sống chủ yếu trong bối cảnh phụng tự và gia đình trong nhiều thế kỷ trước khi được hồi sinh như ngôn ngữ nói vào cuối thế kỷ mười chín. Những gì được lưu giữ trong các nghi lễ gia đình đó — từ vựng, cú pháp, nhịp điệu của lời cầu nguyện — cho những người hồi sinh có thứ cụ thể để xây dựng. Ngược lại, một ngôn ngữ đã bị thay thế hoàn toàn chỉ có thể được tái dựng, và tái dựng luôn kém hơn bản gốc.

Câu trả lời trung thực, vậy thì, là một ngôn ngữ chỉ được nói ở nhà không sống cũng không chết — nó đang ngủ yên. Liệu nó còn ngủ hay thức dậy phụ thuộc ít vào bản thân ngôn ngữ hơn là vào việc cộng đồng có quyết định, tập thể, tạo ra một không gian cho nó vượt ra ngoài căn bếp hay không. Những hộ gia đình giữ lấy các ngôn ngữ thiểu số không phải đang bảo tồn hiện vật bảo tàng; họ đang giữ một cánh cửa mở. Liệu có ai chọn bước qua đó hay không là một câu hỏi hoàn toàn khác.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft correctly to front the key reason?
options: What keeps a language breathing inside four walls is not grammar but intimacy.
It is what keeps language in the walls that is grammar not intimacy.
What is language keeping inside four walls not grammar it intimacy.
The thing what keeps language is not grammar but the intimacy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X is Y' delays the key noun until the end for emphasis. Only option A has the correct structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key noun at the very end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces a concession most naturally at B2 level?
options: Granted, a language heard only at the dinner table is a fragile thing.
Even though granted is the dinner language fragile thing.
Of course but however a fragile dinner language is.
Conceding the dinner table language it is fragile yes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' cleanly introduces an opposing point before the writer returns to their argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the simplest, most natural opener that admits the other view.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the passive correctly to background the agent?
options: People kept Hebrew alive in liturgical contexts.
Hebrew was kept alive largely in liturgical and domestic contexts.
Hebrew kept alive was largely in liturgical contexts.
It was Hebrew that people largely kept alive in contexts liturgical.
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive 'was kept alive' foregrounds the language rather than the unnamed people who maintained it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option foregrounds Hebrew, not the people?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the most precise meaning of 'intergenerational transmission'?
options: Sending a text message to a grandparent
The process by which language or culture is passed from one generation to the next
A legal document transferring property between family members
The biological inheritance of language ability
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Intergenerational transmission' is the sociolinguistic term for passing cultural or linguistic practices across generations.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'generation' and 'transmission' each mean.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges a claim most appropriately?
options: Passive knowledge always definitely fades within one generation.
Passive knowledge tends to fade unless something gives the language a reason to be spoken aloud.
Passive knowledge will 100% fade if people don't speak.
Passive knowledge is fading always unless people speak it always.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tends to fade unless …' adds a condition and hedges the absolute, which is more accurate and credible.
mcQuestions.hint: Which adds a condition and avoids an absolute claim?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best fills the gap: 'A language that has been entirely replaced can only be ___, and reconstruction is always poorer than the original.'
options: improvised
reconstructed
translated
invented
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reconstructed' pairs with the noun 'reconstruction' used later in the same sentence, creating lexical cohesion.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the word that echoes the noun 'reconstruction' used later in the sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals adding a further point after an example?
options: In conclusion, Hebrew…
On the other hand, Hebrew…
What was preserved in those domestic rituals — vocabulary, syntax, the cadence of prayer — gave revivalists something concrete to build on.
Anyway, Hebrew…
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C develops the example with concrete detail rather than using a generic transition.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option stays specific rather than using a vague connector?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which collocation is most natural in academic/argumentative writing?
options: do a space
carve out a space
make a space happen
build out a space
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Carve out a space' is the standard collocation meaning to create room for something in a resistant context.
mcQuestions.hint: Which verb collocates most naturally with 'out a space'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a participle clause as a parenthetical?
options: Welsh reversed this drift partly because road signs and school curricula gave it a public function again.
Welsh reversed this drift, giving it a public function, school curricula being road signs.
Welsh, reversed this drift partly, because road signs gave again a public function.
Welsh was reversed this drift partly road signs school curricula gave.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A is grammatically clean; the participle-clause version would need careful restructuring. At B2, a clean 'because' clause is equally valid.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence is grammatically correct and clear?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which metaphor does the sample essay use for households that preserve minority languages?
options: A museum exhibit
A seed
Holding a door open
Both B and C
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'the seed from which revivals have been grown' and 'holding a door open' as two separate metaphors.
mcQuestions.hint: Read the final two paragraphs carefully for images.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which opening avoids AI-style filler and starts with a concrete image?
options: In today's modern world, language is very important.
Throughout history, people have always spoken languages.
In the Lima suburb of San Juan de Miraflores, Quechua is not spoken in the market.
It is important to note that languages can disappear.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C opens with a specific location and concrete observation, which grounds the reader immediately.
mcQuestions.hint: Concrete place + fact beats generic platitude.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means a language variety used for close, personal relationships?
options: prestige language
formal register
intimate register
dominant language
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Intimate register' refers to the variety of a language used in close personal contexts, such as with family.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase contains the word that means 'close/personal'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'neither … nor …' as a balanced structure?
options: A language spoken only at home is neither alive nor dead — it is dormant.
A language spoken only at home is neither alive or dead it dormant.
A language neither alive spoken at home or dead dormant it is.
Neither alive a language spoken at home nor dead is dormant.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither … nor …' requires parallel structure: 'neither alive nor dead' pairs two adjectives correctly.
mcQuestions.hint: Both items after 'neither' and 'nor' must be the same grammatical type.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best replaces 'recede' in the sentence 'The language has receded from every public surface'?
options: expanded
withdrawn
exploded
arrived
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Withdrawn' is the closest synonym to 'receded' in this context — both imply pulling back or shrinking away.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word means to pull back or shrink away?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps the B2 register throughout?
options: Quechua is like super gone from the streets but grandmas still speak it lol.
The language has receded from every public surface and survived, somehow, behind a door.
Quechua went away from the streets and grandmas just talk it at home ya know.
Languages such as Quechua, they are gone from streets but not from homes!!
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B maintains a formal, measured tone with precise vocabulary ('receded', 'public surface') without being stiff.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, exclamation marks, and informal contractions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'passive competence' mean in the context of language learning?
options: Refusing to study grammar rules
Understanding a language without being able to actively produce it
Translating texts without speaking them aloud
Learning a language through reading only
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Passive competence' means a person can understand (hear, read) a language but cannot or does not actively speak or write it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a language or dialect spoken in your own family but not in public. What specific moment — a word, a phrase, a story — made you realise it was different from the language you use at school or work?
